abstract: This paper investigates the effectiveness of cross-lingual multitask fine-tuning by introducing BLOOMZ and mT0, a family of large language models capable of following human instructions in dozens of languages. To develop these models, we present xP3, a massive collection of prompts spanning 46 languages and 16 diverse NLP tasks. We demonstrate that fine-tuning large-scale multilingual pretrained models on this dataset produces systems with remarkable cross-lingual generalization capabilities. Specifically, our models can follow instructions in languages they were never explicitly trained on for a given task, outperforming existing benchmarks such as mTk-Instruct by significant margins. We further explore the impact of model scaling and task composition, showing that multilingual multitasking enables zero-shot performance that is competitive with much larger monolingual models. Our findings suggest that cross-lingual task generalization is a promising frontier for making AI instructions more accessible globally.
